description: Parse chord symbols covering major, minor, and dominant seventh qualities
detail: A chord symbol is a root note (A–G, with optional b or # accidental) followed by an optional quality suffix. Three qualities are supported in the MVP: major (no suffix), minor ('m'), and dominant seventh ('7'). Examples: C, F#, Bb, Am, C#m, Bbm, G7, Bb7, F#7.
steps: Write a test that parses 'C' and asserts the AST node has root='C' and quality='major'
Write a test that parses 'F#' and asserts root='F#' and quality='major'
Write a test that parses 'Bb' and asserts root='Bb' and quality='major'
Write a test that parses 'Am' and asserts root='A' and quality='minor'
Write a test that parses 'C#m' and asserts root='C#' and quality='minor'
Write a test that parses 'G7' and asserts root='G' and quality='dominant7'
Write a test that parses 'Bb7' and asserts root='Bb' and quality='dominant7'
Write a test that asserts unsupported qualities (e.g. 'Cm7', 'CM7') are rejected by the parser

description: Parse a single bar containing one chord, delimited by single barlines
detail: A bar is written as | chord |. Only the single barline | is supported in the MVP. Each bar contains exactly one chord. Whitespace around the chord is insignificant.
steps: Write a test that parses '| C |' and asserts a bar node with a single chord C major
Write a test that parses '| Am |' and asserts a bar node with a single chord A minor
Write a test that parses '|C|' (no spaces) and produces the same AST as '| C |'
Write a test that parses '|  G7  |' (extra spaces) and produces the same AST as '| G7 |'
Write a test that asserts a bar with no chord (e.g. '| |') is rejected by the parser
Write a test that asserts a bar with two chords (e.g. '| C G |') is rejected by the parser

description: Parse a row of consecutive bars on a single line
detail: A row is a sequence of bars on one line, beginning and ending with a barline. The row produces an ordered list of bar nodes. The opening barline of the row and the closing barline are both represented in the AST.
steps: Write a test that parses '| C | Am | F | G |' and asserts four bar nodes in order
Write a test that parses a single-bar row '| C |' and asserts one bar node
Write a test that parses '| G7 | C | Am | F |' and asserts the chord qualities are correct for each bar
Write a test that asserts a row not starting with '|' is rejected by the parser
Write a test that asserts a row not ending with '|' is rejected by the parser

description: Parse YAML front matter containing title and key fields
detail: Front matter is a YAML block enclosed between '---' delimiters at the start of the file. Two fields are supported in the MVP: 'title' (a string) and 'key' (one of the 12 chromatic note names, with optional accidental: C, C#, Db, D, D#, Eb, E, F, F#, Gb, G, G#, Ab, A, A#, Bb, B). Both fields are optional.
steps: Write a test that parses a front matter block with title and key and asserts the correct values on the song AST node
Write a test that parses a front matter block with only a title (no key) and asserts key is null/undefined
Write a test that parses a front matter block with only a key (no title) and asserts title is null/undefined
Write a test that parses a source file with no front matter at all and asserts the song node has no title or key
Write a test that asserts an invalid key value (e.g. key: 'H') is rejected
Write a test that asserts an invalid key value with a mode (e.g. key: 'Am') is rejected (modes are not in scope for MVP)

description: Parse a complete song: optional front matter followed by one or more rows
detail: A complete .chart file consists of an optional YAML front matter block followed by one or more rows of bars. Blank lines between rows are permitted and ignored. The resulting AST is a song node containing metadata (from front matter) and an ordered list of rows.
steps: Write a test that parses a minimal song (no front matter, one row) and asserts the correct structure
Write a test that parses a song with front matter and multiple rows and asserts metadata and rows are both present
Write a test that parses a song with blank lines between rows and asserts blank lines do not produce extra row nodes

Parse each of the .chart files in packages/grigson/documentation/examples/ and assert that the parser does not throw (note: these files use features beyond MVP scope, so this step only asserts no crash on the supported subset)

description: Render a parsed song AST back to .chart plain text format
detail: The plain text renderer takes a song AST and produces a valid .chart string. The output is a normalised (pretty-printed) form: front matter fields each on their own line, each row of bars on its own line, single spaces around chord names inside bars. The output need not be byte-for-byte identical to the input, but it must parse back to an equivalent AST (round-trip equivalence).
steps: Write a test that renders a song with title and key and asserts the output contains a correctly formatted '---' YAML front matter block
Write a test that renders a single bar '| Am |' and asserts the output is '| Am |' with normalised spacing
Write a test that renders a row of four bars and asserts the output is a single line with all bars separated by barlines
Write a test that renders a multi-row song and asserts each row appears on its own line
Write a round-trip test: parse a source string, render to text, parse the rendered text, and assert the two ASTs are deeply equal
Run the round-trip test against each of the manually authored example songs that use only MVP-supported features

description: Install Prettier and format all non-generated source files
detail: Prettier is added as a devDependency and configured for the project. A pnpm script applies formatting to all files under packages/grigson/src/ except the generated parser output. The .gitignore or a .prettierignore file excludes generated files. All source files are formatted as part of this task.
steps: Install prettier as a devDependency
Add a .prettierrc config file with sensible defaults for a TypeScript project
Add a .prettierignore file excluding packages/grigson/src/parser/generated.js and packages/grigson/src/parser/generated.d.ts
Add a 'format' script to package.json that runs prettier --write on packages/grigson/src/
Run pnpm format and confirm it exits cleanly
Run pnpm test to confirm formatting did not break anything

description: Install ESLint with sensible configuration and fix any existing violations
detail: ESLint is added as a devDependency with a configuration appropriate for a TypeScript ESM project. A lint script is added to package.json. Any violations in existing source files are fixed before the task is marked complete. Generated parser files are excluded from linting.
steps: Install eslint and @eslint/js, typescript-eslint as devDependencies
Create an eslint.config.js (flat config) with rules appropriate for TypeScript and ESM
Exclude packages/grigson/src/parser/generated.js from linting
Add a 'lint' script to package.json that runs eslint on packages/grigson/src/
Run pnpm lint and fix all reported violations
Run pnpm test to confirm fixes did not break anything

description: Extend the parser to support the half-diminished chord quality (m7b5)
detail: The half-diminished quality (written as 'm7b5', e.g. Bm7b5) is needed to represent the ii° chord in harmonic minor keys (e.g. Bm7b5 | E7 | Am). The Quality union type gains a new 'halfDiminished' member. The Peggy grammar is extended to recognise the 'm7b5' suffix. The allowed-start-rules build script is unchanged.
steps: Write a test that parses 'Bm7b5' and asserts root='B' and quality='halfDiminished'
Write a test that parses 'Am7b5' and asserts root='A' and quality='halfDiminished'
Write a test that parses 'C#m7b5' and asserts root='C#' and quality='halfDiminished'
Write a test that the text renderer round-trips a chord with quality halfDiminished back to the 'm7b5' suffix
Add 'halfDiminished' to the Quality union type in packages/grigson/src/parser/types.ts
Extend the Peggy grammar in packages/grigson/src/parser/grammar.pegjs to match the 'm7b5' suffix and emit quality: 'halfDiminished'
Extend the TextRenderer to render halfDiminished chords with the 'm7b5' suffix
Run pnpm build to regenerate the parser, then run pnpm test to confirm all tests pass

description: Implement the pitchClass theory module mapping note spellings to chromatic integers
detail: packages/grigson/src/theory/pitchClass.ts provides: (1) a PitchClass const object mapping canonical flat-side names to integers 0–11; (2) rootToPitchClass(root: string): PitchClass that accepts any valid spelling including enharmonic equivalents (e.g. 'A#' → 10); (3) ENHARMONIC_PAIRS mapping each sharp spelling to its flat equivalent and vice-versa (C#↔Db, D#↔Eb, F#↔Gb, G#↔Ab, A#↔Bb). Double accidentals (E#, B#, Cb, Fb) are out of scope.
steps: Write tests asserting rootToPitchClass returns the correct integer for every natural note: C=0, D=2, E=4, F=5, G=7, A=9, B=11
Write tests asserting rootToPitchClass handles all five sharp spellings: C#=1, D#=3, F#=6, G#=8, A#=10
Write tests asserting rootToPitchClass handles all five flat spellings: Db=1, Eb=3, Gb=6, Ab=8, Bb=10
Write a test asserting rootToPitchClass('A#') === rootToPitchClass('Bb') (enharmonic equivalence)
Write tests asserting ENHARMONIC_PAIRS maps 'A#' to 'Bb', 'Bb' to 'A#', and similarly for all five pairs
Write a test asserting rootToPitchClass throws (or returns undefined) for an unrecognised input like 'X'
Create packages/grigson/src/theory/pitchClass.ts and implement the PitchClass constants, ENHARMONIC_PAIRS, and rootToPitchClass
Run pnpm test to confirm all tests pass

description: Implement the keys theory module defining diatonic note sets for all 24 in-scope keys
detail: packages/grigson/src/theory/keys.ts provides: (1) a KEYS record keyed by key name (e.g. 'F', 'Am') with each value containing the 7 diatonic note names as strings; (2) diatonicNotes(key: string): ReadonlySet<string> returning the note set for a given key. All 12 major keys and 10 harmonic minor keys are in scope (G#m and Ebm are excluded because they require double accidentals). The relative field linking each minor key to its relative major (and vice-versa) is also stored, to assist tie-breaking during key detection.
steps: Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('C') returns the set {C, D, E, F, G, A, B}
Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('F') returns {F, G, A, Bb, C, D, E}
Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('G') returns {G, A, B, C, D, E, F#}
Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('Am') returns {A, B, C, D, E, F, G#} (harmonic minor with raised 7th)
Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('Dm') returns {D, E, F, G, A, Bb, C#}

Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('F#') returns {F#, G#, A#, B, C#, D#, E#} — E# is in scope here as a diatonic note of F# major even though it is not a chord root that the normaliser will ever write
Write a test asserting diatonicNotes('Db') returns {Db, Eb, F, Gb, Ab, Bb, C}
Write a test asserting diatonicNotes for an unknown key throws or returns null
Write a test asserting KEYS contains exactly 22 entries (12 major + 10 minor)
Create packages/grigson/src/theory/keys.ts and implement KEYS and diatonicNotes
Run pnpm test to confirm all tests pass

description: Key detector — Category 1: unambiguous major keys
detail: Create packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts and implement the basic scoring pass: for each of the 22 in-scope keys count how many chord roots (converted to pitch class via rootToPitchClass) are diatonic to that key; return the highest-scoring key name. This initial implementation does not yet need V7 weighting or tie-breaking — the Category 1 progressions are unambiguous enough to be decided by score alone.
steps: Write test T1-a: detectKey([F, Bb, C, F]) === 'F' (F major I-IV-V-I)
Write test T1-b: detectKey([G, D, Em, C]) === 'G' (G major I-V-vi-IV)
Write test T1-c: detectKey([Bb, Eb, F, Bb]) === 'Bb' (Bb major I-IV-V)
Write test T1-d: detectKey([E, A, B, E]) === 'E' (E major I-IV-V; B must not become Cb)
Write test T1-e: detectKey([F#, B, C#, F#]) === 'F#' (F# major I-IV-V)
Create packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts and implement detectKey with diatonic-root scoring
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 1 tests pass

description: Key detector — Category 3: major/minor disambiguation
detail: Extend detectKey with relative-major/minor tie-breaking. When a major key and its relative harmonic minor are tied on diatonic score, apply (in order): (1) V7 of harmonic minor present → minor; (2) last-chord-as-tonic — if the last chord root matches a key's tonic, prefer that key; (3) first-chord-as-tonic (weaker); (4) prefer-major as final fallback. The half-diminished quality (Bm7b5) on the ii° degree is a strong minor signal.
steps: Write test T3-a: detectKey([Am, F, C, G7]) === 'C' (G7 = V7 of C major)
Write test T3-b: detectKey([Am, Dm, E7, Am]) === 'Am' (E7 = V7 of A harmonic minor)
Write test T3-c: detectKey([Am, F, C, G]) === 'C' (last chord G → C major via last-chord heuristic)
Write test T3-d: detectKey([C, Am, F, Am]) === 'Am' (last chord Am → A minor)
Write test T3-e: detectKey([Am, F, C, Am]) === 'Am' (first and last both Am)
Write test T3-f: detectKey([C, Am, F, Am]) === 'Am' (first C, last Am — last wins)
Write test T3-g: detectKey([Bm7b5, E7, Am, Am]) === 'Am' (ii° + V7 = strongest minor signal)
Extend packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts with V7/harmonic-minor detection and last/first-chord tiebreaking
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 1 and Category 3 tests pass

description: Key detector — Category 4: V7 as a strong tonic anchor
detail: Extend detectKey so that a dominant-7th chord whose root is scale-degree V of a candidate key receives a heavy bonus (e.g. 5× the normal diatonic point), making V7 a near-definitive signal. This weight must be strong enough to resolve ties in Category 4 progressions but must not override a conclusive Category 1 majority.
steps: Write test T4-a: detectKey([Dm, G7, C]) === 'C' (ii-V-I; G7 = V7 of C)
Write test T4-b: detectKey([F, Bb, C7, F]) === 'F' (C7 = V7 of F major)
Write test T4-c: detectKey([D#, A#, F7, A#]) === 'Bb' (F7 = V7 of Bb; D#→Eb, A#→Bb after key confirmed)
Extend packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts to apply V7 dominant weighting during the scoring pass
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 1, 3, and 4 tests pass

description: Key detector — Category 6: graceful degradation with sparse data
detail: Extend detectKey to handle sparse chord lists and the optional declaredKey parameter. Rules: (1) if declaredKey is among the top-scoring candidates, return it unchanged; (2) only override declaredKey if it scores zero (no diatonic overlap); (3) always return a key if any candidate scores > 0 — never return null when there is any match; (4) return null only when no chord root matches any candidate key.
steps: Write test T6-a: detectKey([C]) === 'C' (single chord, no declared key; best-guess C major)
Write test T6-b: detectKey([G, D], 'D') === 'D' (declared key D is a valid candidate — preserve it)
Write test T6-c: detectKey([G, D], 'E') === 'G' or 'D' but not 'E' (E scores zero — correct the declared key)
Write test T6-d: detectKey([F, Gm, C]) === 'F' (three chords uniquely identifying F major)
Extend packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts to accept and honour the declaredKey parameter
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 1, 3, 4, and 6 tests pass

description: Key detector — Category 7: robustness to non-diatonic and chromatic chords
detail: Verify that detectKey handles borrowed and chromatic chord roots without being derailed. A single non-diatonic chord in an otherwise clear key should not change the result. A fully chromatic progression with no majority key should return null.
steps: Write test T7-a: detectKey([C, F, Ab, G]) === 'C' (Ab is chromatic/borrowed bVI; C F G anchor C major)
Write test T7-b: detectKey([C, A7, Dm, G7, C]) === 'C' (A7 is secondary dominant V7/ii; overall key C)
Write test T7-c: detectKey([C, Db, D, Eb]) === null (chromatic ascent — no key fits majority)
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 1, 3, 4, 6, and 7 tests pass (no implementation changes expected)

description: Key detector — Category 9: enharmonic boundary keys (Db/C#, Gb/F#)
detail: Extend detectKey with an optional config parameter { fSharpOrGFlat?: 'f-sharp' | 'g-flat' } (default 'f-sharp'). For all keys except the F#/Gb pair, prefer the flat-side spelling when both enharmonic equivalents score equally. For F#/Gb specifically, use the config option to break the tie.
steps: Write test T9-a: detectKey([Db, Ab, Bbm, Gb]) === 'Db' (all chords diatonic to Db major; stay on flat side)
Write test T9-b: detectKey([Gb, Db, Ebm, Ab]) === 'Gb' (prefer flat spelling over F# major)
Write test T9-c default: detectKey([F#, C#, D#m, B]) === 'F#' (default fSharpOrGFlat = 'f-sharp')
Write test T9-c config: detectKey([F#, C#, D#m, B], null, { fSharpOrGFlat: 'g-flat' }) === 'Gb'
Extend packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts to accept the config parameter and apply the F#/Gb tiebreak rule
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 1, 3, 4, 6, 7, and 9 tests pass

description: normaliseSong — Category 2: enharmonic correction of diatonic chord roots
detail: Create packages/grigson/src/theory/normalise.ts exporting normaliseSong(song: Song, config?: { fSharpOrGFlat?: 'f-sharp' | 'g-flat' }): Song. The initial implementation calls detectKey and rewrites every chord root that is diatonic to the detected key but spelled with the wrong enharmonic. Non-diatonic (borrowed) roots are left untouched. The function must be pure: it returns a new Song and never mutates its input.
steps: Write test T2-a: | F | A# | F | C | → A# rewritten to Bb; key detected as F
Write test T2-b: | Bb | D# | F | Bb | → D# rewritten to Eb; key detected as Bb
Write test T2-c: two rows each containing A# in F major — both instances rewritten to Bb
Write test T2-d: | F | A# | F | Bb | — A# rewritten to Bb, existing Bb unchanged
Write test T2-e: | C# | F# | G# | C# | → all three roots rewritten to Db Gb Ab Db; key detected as Db
Write test T2-f: | D# | A# | F | D# | → D#→Eb, A#→Bb; key detected as Bb
Write a test asserting normaliseSong returns a new Song object (not the same reference as the input)
Write a test asserting normaliseSong is idempotent: calling it twice produces the same output as calling it once
Create packages/grigson/src/theory/normalise.ts and implement normaliseSong with diatonic-root enharmonic rewriting
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 2 tests pass

description: normaliseSong — Category 5: front matter key field read and write
detail: Extend normaliseSong so that after detecting the canonical key it updates the song's front matter: if the key field is already correct, leave it; if it is wrong (i.e. not among the top-scoring candidates), replace it; if it is absent, add it. The chord roots are also corrected as in Cat 2.
steps: Write test T5-a: song with key: F and chords | F | Bb | C | F | — key field unchanged, no chord changes
Write test T5-b: song with key: G and chords | F | Bb | C | F | — key field corrected to F
Write test T5-c: song with no front matter and chords | G | D | Em | C | — key: G added to front matter
Write test T5-d: song with key: C and chords | Bb | F | C | Gm | — key corrected to F (Bb and Gm not diatonic to C)
Extend normaliseSong to read and write the song's key field in front matter
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 2 and Category 5 tests pass

description: normaliseSong — Category 8: borrowed chords and secondary dominants
detail: Extend normaliseSong with a next-chord borrowing heuristic for non-diatonic roots: assume the chord is a secondary dominant (V or V7) and look at the following chord to determine the correct spelling. When the heuristic is inconclusive, fall back to preferring the flat spelling for pitch classes 1, 3, 6, 8, 10. Chord roots that are already correctly spelled must not be disturbed.
steps: Write test T8-a: | F | Bb | Eb7 | Ab | — Eb7 is V7 of Ab (next chord); spelling Eb7 is correct (not D#7)
Write test T8-b: | C | A7 | Dm | G7 | C | — A7 root stays A (diatonic to C major as vi); no root rewrite
Write test T8-c: | C | Bb | F | C | — Bb is borrowed bVII in C major; stays Bb (not A#); key stays C
Write test T8-d: | C | G | Ab | Bb | C | — Ab and Bb are borrowed bVI/bVII in C; both spellings preserved; key stays C
Extend packages/grigson/src/theory/normalise.ts with next-chord borrowing analysis and flat-fallback for non-diatonic roots
Run pnpm test to confirm all Category 2, 5, and 8 tests pass

description: Set up the CLI entry point, shebang, and subcommand dispatch
detail: Create packages/grigson/src/cli.ts as the CLI entry point with a Node.js shebang. Add a 'bin' field to packages/grigson/package.json pointing to packages/grigson/dist/cli.js. Install a lightweight argument-parsing library. Implement top-level subcommand dispatch and --help output. Unknown subcommands should exit with a non-zero code and a useful error message.
steps: Install a lightweight argument-parsing library (e.g. minimist or commander) as a dependency
Create packages/grigson/src/cli.ts with a #!/usr/bin/env node shebang and top-level subcommand dispatch
Add a 'bin' field to packages/grigson/package.json: { "grigson": "./dist/cli.js" }
Ensure the TypeScript build includes packages/grigson/src/cli.ts and emits packages/grigson/dist/cli.js
Implement --help / -h output that lists available subcommands and exits with code 0
Write a test asserting that invoking the CLI with an unknown subcommand exits with a non-zero code
Write a test asserting that invoking the CLI with --help exits with code 0 and prints usage
Run pnpm build and confirm packages/grigson/dist/cli.js is produced with the shebang line intact

description: Implement the `grigson normalise` subcommand
detail: The normalise subcommand reads a .chart file (from a file argument or stdin), runs normaliseSong, and writes the result to stdout. Supports --key to override key detection, --enharmonic to control F#/Gb preference, and -i/--in-place to edit the file on disk instead of writing to stdout.
steps: Write a test asserting that `grigson normalise file.chart` writes normalised .chart text to stdout
Write a test asserting that `grigson normalise` with no file argument reads from stdin
Write a test asserting that --key <key> overrides key detection (e.g. --key Am)
Write a test asserting that --enharmonic g-flat is forwarded to normaliseSong config
Write a test asserting that -i edits the file in place and produces no stdout
Write a test asserting that -i with no file argument exits with a non-zero code and an error message
Implement the normalise subcommand handler in packages/grigson/src/cli.ts using parseSong, normaliseSong, and TextRenderer
Run pnpm build && pnpm test to confirm all tests pass

description: Implement the `grigson render` subcommand for plain-text output
detail: The render subcommand reads a .chart file (from a file argument or stdin), parses it, and writes the rendered output to stdout. The only supported format in this task is 'text' (the default). An unknown --format value should exit with a non-zero code. Unlike normalise, render does not run normaliseSong — it is a pure parse-and-render operation.
steps: Write a test asserting that `grigson render file.chart` writes plain-text .chart to stdout
Write a test asserting that `grigson render` with no file argument reads from stdin
Write a test asserting that `grigson render --format text` behaves identically to the default
Write a test asserting that `grigson render --format svg` exits with a non-zero code and a 'not yet implemented' message
Write a test asserting that `grigson render --format unknown` exits with a non-zero code
Implement the render subcommand handler in packages/grigson/src/cli.ts using parseSong and TextRenderer
Run pnpm build && pnpm test to confirm all tests pass

description: Extend detectKey with ending-key tie-breaking
detail: Add a tie-breaking rule to detectKey so that when the last chord's root matches a candidate key's tonic and a tie exists on diatonic score, the key containing the last chord as tonic is preferred. This makes detectKey handle sections that open in one key and cadence in another (e.g. [Cm, D7, Gm, A7, Dm] → 'Dm'). This is an additive change to keyDetector.ts; no existing tests should break.
steps: Write a test asserting detectKey([Cm, D7, Gm, A7, Dm]) === 'Dm' (Whisper Not A-section ending; Dm is the cadence tonic)
Write a test asserting detectKey([Am, F, C, G]) === 'C' (last chord G, not a tonic match — existing last-chord heuristic still returns C)
Write a test asserting detectKey([C, Am, F, Am]) === 'Am' (last chord Am → minor wins over C major)
Confirm the new test for ending-key-wins fails before any implementation
Extend detectKey in packages/grigson/src/theory/keyDetector.ts: after scoring, if the last chord root matches a top-scoring candidate's tonic, promote that candidate to first
Run pnpm test to confirm all new and existing tests pass

description: Extend the parser and AST to support labelled sections
detail: A section label is a line of the form [Label] (a non-empty string surrounded by square brackets) that may appear between rows in the song body. Section labels group the rows that follow them. The Song AST gains a sections: Section[] field alongside (or replacing) rows: Row[]. The Section interface has { type: 'section'; label: string | null; rows: Row[] } where label is null for the implicit first section when no label precedes it. The Peggy grammar, type definitions, TextRenderer, and all downstream consumers must be updated consistently. The plain-text renderer outputs section labels on their own line, e.g. [Verse 1].
steps: Add a Section interface to packages/grigson/src/parser/types.ts: { type: 'section'; label: string | null; rows: Row[] } and change Song.rows to Song.sections: Section[]
Extend the Peggy grammar in packages/grigson/src/parser/grammar.pegjs to parse section labels ([Label]) as top-level song body elements and group subsequent rows into Section nodes
Run pnpm build to regenerate the parser and resolve any TypeScript errors
Write a test that parses a song with no section labels and asserts Song.sections has one entry with label = null containing all rows

Write a test that parses '[Verse]
| C | Am |
[Chorus]
| F | G |' and asserts two Section nodes with the correct labels and rows
Write a test that parses a song where a section label appears after blank lines and asserts blank lines do not produce extra section nodes
Update TextRenderer to emit section labels (e.g. '[Verse]\n') before each section's rows, skipping the label line when label is null
Write a round-trip test: parse a song with section labels, render to text, parse again, and assert the two ASTs are deeply equal
Update any code in normalise.ts, keyDetector.ts, or the CLI that currently iterates Song.rows to iterate Song.sections[n].rows instead
Run pnpm test to confirm all existing and new tests pass

description: Refactor normaliseSong to operate per section with independent key detection
detail: Extract a normaliseSection(chords: Chord[]): { homeKey: string | null; chords: Chord[] } function that detects the key for a given chord sequence and rewrites diatonic roots. Refactor normaliseSong to iterate Song.sections and call normaliseSection on each section's chords independently. The public API of normaliseSong is unchanged. The front-matter key field is set to the home key of the first section.
steps: Write a test asserting that a two-section song (verse in E major, chorus in A major) produces correct enharmonic spellings for each section independently
Write a test asserting that a single-section song (no labels) produces the same result as the current normaliseSong
Write a test asserting the front-matter key is set to the home key of the first section
Extract normaliseSection from normaliseSong in packages/grigson/src/theory/normalise.ts
Update normaliseSong to call normaliseSection per section and merge the results into a new Song
Export normaliseSection from packages/grigson/src/index.ts
Run pnpm test to confirm all existing normalise tests and new section tests pass

description: Implement harmonicAnalysis with 2-5-1 and 5-1 pattern detection
detail: Create packages/grigson/src/theory/harmonicAnalysis.ts. Define the AnnotatedChord interface: { chord: Chord; homeKey: string; currentKey: string; currentKeyCandidates: string[] }. Export analyseHarmony(chords: Chord[], homeKey: string): AnnotatedChord[]. The function performs a left-to-right sliding window scan. Pattern priority (highest to lowest): (1) 2-5-1: minor/half-diminished a whole step below a dom7, followed by a chord a fifth below the dom7 — assign currentKey of all three to the resolved tonic; (2) 5-1: dom7 followed by a chord a fifth below — assign currentKey of both to the resolved tonic. Chords not matched by any pattern receive currentKey = homeKey.
steps: Write a test asserting analyseHarmony([C, F, G, C], 'C') returns all chords with currentKey = 'C'
Write a test for 5-1: analyseHarmony([C, A7, Dm, G7, C], 'C') assigns A7 and Dm to currentKey = 'Dm', G7 and final C to currentKey = 'C'
Write a test for 2-5-1: analyseHarmony([C, Bbm, Eb7, Abmaj7, G7, C], 'C') assigns Bbm, Eb7, Abmaj7 to currentKey = 'Ab' and remaining chords to 'C'
Write a test for 2-5-1 in Db: analyseHarmony([F, Ebm, Ab7, Dbmaj7, C7, F], 'F') assigns Ebm, Ab7, Dbmaj7 to currentKey = 'Db'
Write a test asserting 2-5-1 takes priority over 5-1 when both patterns overlap at the same position

description: Extend harmonic analysis to handle isolated borrowed chords via circle-of-fifths
detail: Extend analyseHarmony so that a chord not covered by a 2-5-1 or 5-1 pattern, and whose root is not diatonic to homeKey, gets a currentKey derived from circle-of-fifths distance. Collect all 22 in-scope keys that contain the chord's pitch class as a diatonic note. Rank them by fewest steps from homeKey on the circle of fifths (relative major/minor counts as 0 extra steps). The closest key becomes currentKey; all candidates are stored in currentKeyCandidates.
steps: Write a test for isolated bVI: analyseHarmony([C, Ab, G], 'C') assigns Ab a currentKey consistent with the closest key containing pitch class 8 to C (expected: 'Cm' or 'Am')
Write a test for isolated bVII: analyseHarmony([C, Bb, F, G], 'C') assigns Bb currentKey = 'F' (1 step from C on the circle)
Write a test for isolated Bb7: analyseHarmony([C, Bb7, C], 'C') assigns Bb7 currentKey = 'F' (closest key containing Bb as dom7 root is F major, 1 step from C)
Write a test asserting that for an unambiguously diatonic chord the currentKey remains homeKey and is not overridden by the circle-of-fifths logic

description: Wire harmonic analysis into normalisation for correct borrowed-chord spelling
detail: Extend normaliseSection to call analyseHarmony and use each chord's currentKey (rather than homeKey) when rewriting enharmonic spellings. A chord root diatonic to currentKey is spelled according to currentKey's diatonic note set. A chord root not diatonic to any candidate key is left unchanged. This supersedes the existing next-chord borrowing heuristic in normalise.ts.
steps: Write a test: normaliseSong with [C, Bbm, Eb7, Abmaj7, G7, C] produces Bbm, Eb7, Abmaj7 (not A#m, D#7, G#maj7)

Write a test: normaliseSong with [C, A7, Dm, G7, C] does not rewrite A (A is diatonic root and the 7 quality does not affect root spelling)
Write a test: normaliseSong with [Dm7b5, G7, Am, Am] produces Dm7b5 (not C##m7b5 or similar nonsense)

description: Implement transposition using a shift-then-normalise approach at section level
detail: Create packages/grigson/src/theory/transpose.ts exporting three functions. transposeSection(chords: Chord[], semitones: number): Chord[] shifts every chord root's pitch class by the given semitone delta (mod 12), preserving chord quality, then delegates all enharmonic spelling decisions to normaliseSection. transposeSong(song: Song, semitones: number): Song applies transposeSection to each section's chords and updates the front-matter key field to the resulting home key. transposeSongToKey(song: Song, targetKey: string): Song computes the semitone interval from the detected home key of the first section to targetKey, then calls transposeSong. No spelling logic lives in transpose.ts — the correct note names emerge entirely from normalisation.

Write a test: transposeSongToKey(song in C, 'G') produces the same result as transposeSong(song, +7)

Accepts --raise <n>, --lower <n>, or --to <key>; exactly one must be provided. --raise and --lower take positive integers, avoiding negative-number flag-parsing ambiguity.

description: Extend parser and renderer with diminished, maj7, min7, and dim7 qualities
detail:
Add four new members to the Quality union type and update all layers that touch Quality. See project/plans/chord-qualities.md — Phase 1 for the full rationale and grammar ordering rules.

New qualities: 'diminished' (suffix 'dim'), 'maj7' (suffixes 'maj7' and 'M7' on input, canonical output 'maj7'), 'min7' (suffix 'm7'), 'dim7' (suffix 'dim7').

Grammar ordering in the Quality rule is critical — longer alternatives must precede shorter ones to avoid premature matches: m7b5 > maj7 > M7 > m7 > m, and dim7 > dim.

Write parser tests: 'Dm7' → quality='min7', 'Am7' → quality='min7', 'Bm7' → quality='min7'

description: Extend the TextMate grammar to highlight the four new chord qualities
detail: The chord quality regex in packages/textmate-grammar/grigson.tmLanguage.json currently matches only 'm7b5', 'm', and '7'. It must be extended to also match 'dim7', 'maj7', 'M7', 'm7', and 'dim' — the five suffix forms added in chord-qualities-parse-render. Regex ordering is critical: longer alternatives must precede shorter ones to avoid premature matches (e.g. 'dim7' before 'dim', 'maj7' before 'm7', 'm7b5' before 'm7' before 'm'). The correct order is: m7b5|maj7|M7|dim7|m7|dim|m|7. The vscode-extension copy must be kept in sync. The syntax-test.chart example file should be updated to include chords using the new qualities.

description: Extend the parser to recognise time signature annotations in bars
detail: A time signature is written as (N/D) immediately after a barline and before the bar's chord content, e.g. | (4/4) C | or | (3/4) Am |. It applies to that bar and remains in effect for all subsequent bars until a new time signature is encountered. The Bar AST node gains an optional 'timeSignature?: { numerator: number; denominator: number }' field which is set only on bars that explicitly declare one — tracking the effective time signature across bars is the responsibility of later passes, not the parser. The TextRenderer emits '(N/D) ' before the first chord of any bar that declares a time signature. This task does not change the Bar's chord content structure — it only adds the time signature field alongside the existing 'chord: Chord' field.

description: Extend Bar to hold a sequence of chords and dots, replacing the single-chord field
detail: Replace 'Bar.chord: Chord' with 'Bar.slots: BeatSlot[]'. A BeatSlot is either a ChordSlot ({ type: 'chord'; chord: Chord }) or a DotSlot ({ type: 'dot' }). The grammar is updated to parse one or more chord-or-dot tokens inside a bar. A bar must contain at least one ChordSlot (a bar of only dots is invalid). All existing consumers of bar.chord are updated: TextRenderer and HtmlRenderer join slots with spaces rendering dots as '.'; normalise.ts, keyDetector callers, and harmonicAnalysis callers extract chords by filtering slots for ChordSlots. All bar-parsing tests are updated from bar.chord to bar.slots[0].chord (or the equivalent typed access). This is a breaking AST change — TypeScript compilation errors are the guide for finding all affected sites. This task depends on time-signature-parsing (the grammar changes are additive to that task's output).

description: Semantic validator check: warn when a mode-2 bar's slot count does not match its effective time signature
detail: After a successful parse, validate() runs a new semanticChecks(song: Song): Diagnostic[] pass. The first semantic check is beat balance. For each bar in the song (iterating sections → rows → bars), track the effective time signature by carrying forward the last declared timeSignature, defaulting to { numerator: 4, denominator: 4 } if none has been declared. A bar is in mode 2 if it contains at least one DotSlot. For mode-2 bars, the expected slot count is the effective time signature's numerator. Emit a severity: 'warning' Diagnostic if the actual slot count differs from the expected count — both underfilled (slot count < numerator, trailing dots implied but source is ambiguous) and overfilled (slot count > numerator, cannot be corrected by any trailing-dot rule) produce warnings. Mode-1 bars (no DotSlots) are never warned about — the even-split rule is always unambiguous. The Diagnostic range should point at the bar's position; since Peggy location data is not currently stored on AST nodes, use the position of the enclosing row as an approximation for now. semanticChecks is an unexported helper inside validator.ts, keeping validate() as the single public entry point.

Write a test: validate('| (4/4) C . . . . . G |') returns one Diagnostic with severity 'warning' — 7 slots in 4/4, overfilled
Write a test: validate('| C G |') returns [] — mode 1 (no dots), no warning regardless of how many chords
Write a test: validate('| (4/4) C | (3/4) Am . G |') returns [] — second bar is mode 2 with exactly 3 slots in 3/4
Write a test: validate('| (3/4) C | Am . G |') returns [] — second bar inherits the (3/4) time signature from the first bar and has 3 slots

description: Add TextMate pattern for time-signature tokens (e.g. (4/4))
detail: The Peggy grammar's TimeSignatureToken rule matches '(numerator/denominator)' tokens that appear at the start of a bar. The TextMate grammar has no pattern for this, so the entire token is unstyled. Add a pattern that captures the parens, digits, and slash separately.
steps: Add a 'time-signature' entry to the TextMate repository with a match like '(\\()(\\d+)(/)(\\d+)(\\))' and captures: 1=punctuation.section.time-signature.begin.grigson, 2=constant.numeric.grigson, 3=punctuation.separator.grigson, 4=constant.numeric.grigson, 5=punctuation.section.time-signature.end.grigson
Include '#time-signature' in the main pattern list, before '#chord'
Smoke-test with a bar like '| (3/4) Am | (6/8) C |' and confirm the token is highlighted

description: Fix tree-sitter bar_tail to support BeatSlotList: multiple beat slots and the dot repeat token
detail: The Peggy grammar's BarTail contains a BeatSlotList — a sequence of one or more BeatSlots, each of which is either a Chord or a '.' (dot). The tree-sitter grammar's bar_tail only allows a single $.chord, making bars with multiple chords or dots unparseable. Introduce a beat_slot rule (chord | dot) and update bar_tail to use repeat1($.beat_slot).
steps: Add a 'dot' rule: dot: (_$) => '.'
Add a 'beat_slot' rule: beat_slot: ($) => choice($.chord, $.dot)
Update 'bar_tail': bar_tail: ($) => seq(optional($.time_signature), repeat1($.beat_slot), $.barline)
Regenerate the C parser: run 'tree-sitter generate' in packages/tree-sitter-grammar/
Update or add corpus tests in test/corpus/ covering: a bar with a single chord, a bar with multiple chords, a bar with chord-dot-chord, a bar with a leading time signature
Run 'tree-sitter test' and confirm all tests pass
Update queries/highlights.scm: add captures for (dot) @punctuation.separator and (beat_slot) if needed
Commit grammar.js, regenerated src/parser.c, updated corpus tests, and updated highlights.scm

description: Distinguish quoted vs unquoted frontmatter values in tree-sitter grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar parses FrontMatterValue as either a double-quoted string (stripping the quotes) or a plain unquoted value. The tree-sitter grammar uses a single /[^\r\n]+/ regex for frontmatter_value, capturing quotes as raw text with no structural distinction. Add quoted_string and unquoted_value alternatives so consumers and highlight queries can differentiate the two forms.
steps: Add a 'quoted_string' rule matching double-quoted content: quoted_string: (_$) => /"[^"]*"/
Add an 'unquoted_value' rule: unquoted_value: (_$) => /[^\r\n"]+/
Replace 'frontmatter_value' with a choice: frontmatter_value: ($) => choice($.quoted_string, $.unquoted_value)
Regenerate the C parser: run 'tree-sitter generate'
Update corpus tests: add cases for 'title: "My Song"' (quoted) and 'key: C' (unquoted) and confirm each produces the right child node type
Update queries/highlights.scm: scope (quoted_string) as @string and (unquoted_value) as @string
Run 'tree-sitter test' and confirm all tests pass
Commit grammar.js, regenerated src/parser.c, updated corpus tests, and updated highlights.scm

description: Add E#, B#, Cb, and Fb to the pitchClass NOTE_MAP
detail: Several keys in KEYS already include E# (F#m), B# (C#m), and the new Abm/Ebm entries will need Cb and Fb. Currently buildPCToNote silently skips notes not in NOTE_MAP, so those scale degrees become unavailable for normalisation. Adding these single-step enharmonic equivalents to NOTE_MAP allows normalise.ts to spell chords correctly in all affected keys. E# = PC 5, B# = PC 0, Cb = PC 11, Fb = PC 4. Double sharps (F##, C##) are out of scope — they will be handled via pragmatic substitution in the KEYS entries (task minor-keys-add).
steps: Add 'E#': 5 to NOTE_MAP in pitchClass.ts
Add 'B#': 0 to NOTE_MAP in pitchClass.ts
Add 'Cb': 11 to NOTE_MAP in pitchClass.ts
Add 'Fb': 4 to NOTE_MAP in pitchClass.ts
Add unit tests in pitchClass or normalise test files: assert rootToPitchClass('E#') === 5, 'B#' === 0, 'Cb' === 11, 'Fb' === 4
Confirm existing normalise tests still pass (pnpm test)

description: Add G#m, Abm, D#m, and Ebm to the KEYS table as enharmonic pairs
detail: Currently only 10 minor keys are defined. G#m/Abm (5 sharps / 7 flats) and D#m/Ebm (6 sharps / 6 flats) are missing. Both pairs should be supported as enharmonic alternates, mirroring how F#/Gb are handled for major keys. Scale spellings: Abm = [Ab, Bb, Cb, Db, Eb, Fb, G], G#m = [G#, A#, B, C#, D#, E, G] (raised 7th F## replaced by G, the enharmonic natural); Ebm = [Eb, F, Gb, Ab, Bb, Cb, D], D#m = [D#, E#, F#, G#, A#, B, D] (raised 7th C## replaced by D). Relative major: D#m → F# (already in KEYS), Ebm → Gb (already in KEYS), G#m → B (already in KEYS), Abm has no relative in KEYS (Cb major is not defined — leave relative undefined). Add G#m↔Abm and D#m↔Ebm to ENHARMONIC_PAIRS in pitchClass.ts. Depends on minor-keys-exotic-note-names for Cb and Fb to be recognised during normalisation.
steps: Add Abm: { notes: ['Ab', 'Bb', 'Cb', 'Db', 'Eb', 'Fb', 'G'] } to KEYS (no relative — Cb major not in KEYS)
Add G#m: { notes: ['G#', 'A#', 'B', 'C#', 'D#', 'E', 'G'], relative: 'B' } to KEYS (G is pragmatic substitute for F##)
Add Ebm: { notes: ['Eb', 'F', 'Gb', 'Ab', 'Bb', 'Cb', 'D'], relative: 'Gb' } to KEYS
Add D#m: { notes: ['D#', 'E#', 'F#', 'G#', 'A#', 'B', 'D'], relative: 'F#' } to KEYS (D is pragmatic substitute for C##)
Add 'G#m': 'Abm', 'Abm': 'G#m', 'D#m': 'Ebm', 'Ebm': 'D#m' to ENHARMONIC_PAIRS in pitchClass.ts
Write normalisation tests: a chart in G#m normalises correctly (e.g. Ab chord spelled as G#); an Abm chart normalises correctly (e.g. G# chord spelled as Ab); same for D#m/Ebm
Run pnpm test and confirm all tests pass

description: Extend key detection and CLI to handle G#m/Abm and D#m/Ebm enharmonic tiebreaks
detail: Key detection already handles the F#/Gb enharmonic tiebreak via breakFSharpGbTie, which checks chord-root spelling in the input and falls back to a config preference. The same mechanism must be added for G#m/Abm and D#m/Ebm. The chord-spelling heuristic mirrors breakFSharpGbTie: if more chord roots are spelled with flats (Ab, Eb) prefer the flat key; if more are spelled with sharps (G#, D#) prefer the sharp key. Extend DetectKeyConfig with optional 'gSharpOrAFlat' and 'dSharpOrEFlat' preference fields. Expose these in the CLI: extend the --enharmonic flag to accept 'g-sharp'/'a-flat' and 'd-sharp'/'e-flat', or add new flags. Update cli.md and the normalise --help text to document the new options.
steps: Add breakGSharpAbTie(chords, config) in keyDetector.ts mirroring breakFSharpGbTie: count Ab vs G# chord roots; prefer matching spelling; default to the flat side (Abm) if tied
Add breakDSharpEbTie(chords, config) similarly: count Eb vs D# chord roots; default to flat side (Ebm) if tied
Wire both tiebreakers into detectKey: after the F#/Gb tiebreak block, add analogous blocks for G#m/Abm and D#m/Ebm
Extend DetectKeyConfig: add gSharpOrAFlat?: 'g-sharp' | 'a-flat' and dSharpOrEFlat?: 'd-sharp' | 'e-flat'
Extend CLI --enharmonic to accept the four new values, mapping them to the new DetectKeyConfig fields (and keeping the existing f-sharp/g-flat handling intact)
Write key-detection tests: a progression clearly in G#m is detected as G#m; one clearly in Abm is detected as Abm; same for D#m/Ebm
Update cli.md --enharmonic option table to document all six accepted values
Update the --help text in cli.ts normalise section to reflect the new --enharmonic values
Run pnpm test and confirm all tests pass

description: Add getKeyMode/getKeyRoot helpers to keys.ts and refactor all call sites
detail: Currently the codebase identifies minor keys with key.endsWith('m') and extracts the root with key.slice(0, -1). This pattern cannot handle modal key names like 'E dorian'. Add getKeyMode(key): KeyMode (returning 'major' | 'minor' | 'dorian') and getKeyRoot(key): string to keys.ts, then replace every raw endsWith/slice call across keyDetector.ts, harmonicAnalysis.ts, and transpose.ts. This is a pure refactor — no behaviour changes, all existing tests must continue to pass. It is a prerequisite for all other dorian tasks.
steps: Add type KeyMode = 'major' | 'minor' | 'dorian' to keys.ts
Add getKeyMode(key: string): KeyMode — returns 'dorian' if key ends with ' dorian', 'minor' if ends with 'm', else 'major'
Add getKeyRoot(key: string): string — strips ' dorian' (7 chars) or trailing 'm', returns key unchanged for major
Export both from keys.ts

In keyDetector.ts replace: line 30 isMinor = key.endsWith('m') → getKeyMode(key) === 'minor'; line 31 rootName slice → getKeyRoot(key); line 71 minor.slice(0,-1) → getKeyRoot(minor); line 168 bestKey.endsWith('m') → getKeyMode(bestKey) === 'minor'; line 204 key.endsWith('m') ? key.slice → getKeyRoot(key); line 218 key.endsWith('m') → getKeyMode(key) === 'minor'

In harmonicAnalysis.ts replace: lines 28-29 getPosition helper — map minor and dorian to relative major using getKeyMode/getKeyRoot; lines 61-62 buildKeyMaps isMinor → getKeyMode; line 202 !homeKey.endsWith('m') → getKeyMode(homeKey) === 'major'; lines 207/211 !k.endsWith('m') → getKeyMode(k) !== 'minor'
In transpose.ts replace any homeKey.replace(/m$/, '') fallback with getKeyRoot(homeKey)
Run pnpm test — all existing tests must pass with no behaviour change

description: Add 12 dorian mode entries to the KEYS table in keys.ts
detail: Add one KEYS entry per dorian key using the 'X dorian' naming convention. Scale formula: [0,2,3,5,7,9,10] semitones from root (W-H-W-W-W-H-W). The relative field points to the relative major (the major key whose tonic is one whole step below the dorian root, e.g. 'E dorian' → relative: 'D'). This is pure data with no logic changes. Depends on dorian-key-helpers (so getKeyMode/getKeyRoot are available if tests import them). The 12 keys are: C dorian (rel: Bb), D dorian (rel: C), E dorian (rel: D), F dorian (rel: Eb), G dorian (rel: F), A dorian (rel: G), B dorian (rel: A), Bb dorian (rel: Ab), Eb dorian (rel: Db), F# dorian (rel: E), Ab dorian (rel: Gb), C# dorian (rel: B).
steps: Add a '// Dorian mode' comment section to KEYS after the harmonic minor entries
Add all 12 entries with correct notes arrays (natural-minor scale with raised 6th) and relative fields
Verify notes by spot-check: E dorian = [E, F#, G, A, B, C#, D] (same notes as D major); B dorian = [B, C#, D, E, F#, G#, A] (same notes as A major)
Add a keys.test.ts case asserting that diatonicNotes('E dorian') returns the correct set and that 'E dorian' has relative 'D'
Add a test asserting getKeyMode('E dorian') === 'dorian' and getKeyRoot('E dorian') === 'E'
Run pnpm test

description: Add dorian interval/quality tables to keyDetector.ts and update buildKeyScoreMap
detail: Key detection scores each key against the chord sequence using interval + quality-set tables. Add DORIAN_INTERVALS = [0,2,3,5,7,9,10] and DORIAN_DEGREE_QUALITY_SETS (i=minor, ii=minor, bIII=major, IV=major+dom7+maj7, v=minor, vi°=dim+halfDim, bVII=major+dom7+maj7). Update buildKeyScoreMap to select dorian tables when getKeyMode(key) === 'dorian'. After this task, detectKey will score dorian keys but the dorian/relative-major tiebreak is not yet wired (that is task dorian-detection-tiebreak). Depends on dorian-key-helpers and dorian-keys-table.
steps: Add DORIAN_INTERVALS = [0, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10] constant
Add DORIAN_DEGREE_QUALITY_SETS: i=minor/min7, ii=minor/min7, bIII=major/maj7, IV=major/dominant7/maj7, v=minor/min7, vi°=diminished/halfDiminished, bVII=major/dominant7/maj7
Update buildKeyScoreMap: add a third branch for mode === 'dorian' using DORIAN_INTERVALS and DORIAN_DEGREE_QUALITY_SETS

Write keyDetector tests: detectKey([Bm, A, E], undefined) should return 'B dorian' (E major scores 2 for B dorian — diatonic root + quality match — and 0 for B harmonic minor which lacks G#; scoring alone distinguishes them)
Write a test: detectKey([Em, G, D, A], undefined) scores 'E dorian' highly (A major scores 2: diatonic + quality) and 'E harmonic minor' poorly (A has C#, not in E harm. minor)
Run pnpm test

description: Add breakDorianMajorTie and wire it into detectKey
detail: Dorian and its relative major share all seven notes and will score identically for any progression that lacks a V7→I (major) cadence or IV→i (dorian plagal) cadence. Add breakDorianMajorTie(dorian, major, chords) that applies the following tiebreakers in order: (1) V7→I cadence where I is major → relative major; (2) major IV → minor i (dorian plagal cadence) → dorian; (3) quality of last chord (minor tonic → dorian, major tonic → relative major); (4) quality of first chord; (5) default to relative major. Wire it into detectKey after the existing F#/Gb enharmonic block: check if bestKey is dorian and its relative major tied, or if bestKey is major and a dorian on its 2nd degree tied. Depends on dorian-detection-scoring.
steps: Implement breakDorianMajorTie(dorian: string, major: string, chords: Chord[]): string following the priority order in the plan
In detectKey, after the F#/Gb tiebreak block, add: if getKeyMode(bestKey) === 'dorian', check if KEYS[bestKey].relative scored equally → call breakDorianMajorTie
Also add: if getKeyMode(bestKey) === 'major', find any dorian key whose relative === bestKey and which tied on score → call breakDorianMajorTie
Write test: detectKey([Em, G, D, A]) with no declared key → 'E dorian' (A→Em is IV→i dorian plagal cadence)
Write test: detectKey([C, Em, Am, G]) → 'C' (G→C is V→I in C major; major wins tiebreak)
Write test: detectKey([Dm, C, G, Dm]) → 'D dorian' (G→Dm is IV→i; last chord is minor Dm)
Write test: detectKey([Em, G, D, A], 'E dorian') → 'E dorian' (declared key preserved when score > 0, no tiebreak needed)
Run pnpm test

description: Update circleOfFifthsDistance to handle dorian keys
detail: circleOfFifthsDistance maps minor keys to their relative major before computing position. It must do the same for dorian keys — 'E dorian' maps to 'D' (its relative major) for distance computation, giving it COF distance 0 from D major and 0 from B minor (the same family). The change is in the getPosition inner function inside circleOfFifthsDistance. Depends on dorian-key-helpers (so getKeyMode is importable).
steps: In circleOfFifthsDistance, update the getPosition helper: for mode === 'dorian', resolve to KEYS[key].relative (same logic as for minor keys)
The final fallback for keys with no relative (e.g. Abm, whose relative Cb is not in KEYS) should return -1 as before
Write a test: circleOfFifthsDistance('E dorian', 'D') === 0
Write a test: circleOfFifthsDistance('E dorian', 'G') === 1 (G is 1 step from D on the COF)
Write a test: circleOfFifthsDistance('E dorian', 'C') === 2
Run pnpm test

description: Update buildKeyMaps in harmonicAnalysis.ts to include dorian keys
detail: buildKeyMaps builds majorByPC and minorByPC lookup maps used by resolveKey to find the canonical key name for a resolved tonic pitch class. Dorian keys need to be included so that if a chord progression tonicises a dorian centre, the correct key name can be returned. Add a dorianByPC map using the same structure. Update resolveKey to accept an optional mode preference or check context; for now, resolveKey(tonicPC, iMinor=true) prefers harmonic minor (preserving current behaviour), but adds dorian as a fallback when no minor key exists for that PC. Depends on dorian-key-helpers and dorian-keys-table.
steps: In buildKeyMaps, add dorianByPC: Map<number, string> — iterate KEYS entries where getKeyMode(key) === 'dorian', map root PC to key name
Export DORIAN_BY_PC constant alongside MAJOR_BY_PC and MINOR_BY_PC
Update resolveKey: when iChordIsMinor is true, prefer MINOR_BY_PC; if no entry found, fall back to DORIAN_BY_PC (a tonicisation to a dorian centre is better than null)
Write a test: calling analyseHarmony on [Em, A, D, Em] with homeKey 'E dorian' produces currentKey 'E dorian' for all chords (all diatonic)
Run pnpm test

description: Recognise the IV→i dorian plagal cadence in analyseHarmony
detail: The major IV → minor i motion (e.g. G → Dm in D dorian, A → Em in E dorian) is the most characteristic dorian cadence. Currently analyseHarmony only recognises 2-5-1 and 5-1 functional patterns. Add a 3rd pattern — the dorian plagal cadence — that fires only when homeKey is a dorian mode. Pattern: a major chord whose root is a perfect 4th above the dorian tonic, followed by a minor chord whose root IS the dorian tonic. Assign currentKey = homeKey to both chords. This pattern should be checked after 2-5-1 and 5-1 (it is weaker), and only when homeKey is dorian. Depends on dorian-harmonic-key-maps.
steps: After the 5-1 pattern block in analyseHarmony, add a dorian plagal cadence block gated on getKeyMode(homeKey) === 'dorian'
Compute tonicPC = rootToPitchClass(getKeyRoot(homeKey)); ivPC = (tonicPC + 5) % 12
Pattern: chords[i].quality === 'major' && getPC(chords[i]) === ivPC && i+1 < chords.length && chords[i+1].quality === 'minor' && getPC(chords[i+1]) === tonicPC
On match: push both chords annotated with currentKey = homeKey, advance i by 2
Write test: analyseHarmony([G, Dm, C, Dm], 'D dorian') — G→Dm matches plagal cadence; both get currentKey 'D dorian'; C and final Dm also get homeKey
Write test: analyseHarmony([A, Em, D, Em], 'E dorian') — A→Em matches; both get 'E dorian'
Write test: non-dorian homeKey (e.g. 'C') — IV→I (F→C) does NOT trigger the dorian pattern (gate check works)
Run pnpm test

description: Silently ignore `#` comment lines in song body
detail: Add a Comment rule to the PEG grammar so that lines beginning with `#` outside front matter are parsed and discarded. No type or renderer changes needed. See project/plans/parser-next-steps.md for full context.
steps: Add `Comment = "#" $[^\n\r]* Newline { return null; }` rule to grammar.pegjs
Add `Comment` as an alternative in SongBody alongside Newline/SectionLabel/Row
Confirm null values are already filtered in the SongBody action (they are, via the `typeof item !== 'object'` check)
Run `pnpm build` to regenerate generated.js
Add parser tests for a song with comment lines (single comment, comment before section, comment between rows)
Run `pnpm test` and confirm all tests pass

description: Add `-` (min7) and `7b5` (dom7flat5) chord quality suffixes
detail: Extend the Quality rule in the PEG grammar with two new alternatives, add `dom7flat5` to the Quality union type in types.ts, and add a renderer case for it in text.ts. The `-` suffix maps to `min7` (C- = Cm7). The `7b5` suffix maps to a new `dom7flat5` quality. See project/plans/parser-next-steps.md for full context.
steps: Add `"7b5" { return "dom7flat5"; }` to Quality rule in grammar.pegjs, before the `"7"` alternative
Add `"-" { return "min7"; }` to Quality rule in grammar.pegjs, before the `"m"` alternative
Add `'dom7flat5'` to the Quality union type in types.ts
Add `case 'dom7flat5': suffix = '7b5'; break;` to renderChord in text.ts
Run `pnpm build` to regenerate generated.js
Add parser tests: `C-` parses as min7, `G7b5` parses as dom7flat5, round-trip for each
Add renderer tests: `dom7flat5` renders as `7b5`
Run `pnpm test` and confirm all tests pass

description: Support slash chords with explicit bass notes (e.g. F/C, F#/A#, C-/Bb)
detail: Add an optional `bass` field to the Chord interface, add a SlashBass rule to the grammar, and update the renderer to emit the `/bass` suffix when present. See project/plans/parser-next-steps.md for full context.
steps: Add `bass?: string` to the Chord interface in types.ts
Add `SlashBass = "/" bass:Root { return bass; }` rule to grammar.pegjs
Update the Chord rule to capture `bass:SlashBass?` and include it in the returned object when non-null
Run `pnpm build` to regenerate generated.js
Update renderChord in text.ts to append `'/' + chord.bass` when bass is present
Add parser tests: `F/C`, `F#/A#`, `C-/Bb` (requires Task 2 for the `-` quality)
Add renderer tests and round-trip tests for slash chords
Run `pnpm test` and confirm all tests pass

description: Support repeat and multi-barline types: `||`, `||.`, `||:`, `:||`, `:||xN`, `:||:`
detail: Replace the hardcoded `|` tokens in Row and BarTail with OpenBarline/CloseBarline rules. Add BarlineKind and Barline types to types.ts, and update Bar to carry a closeBarline and Row to carry an openBarline. Update the renderer to emit the correct symbols. Existing parser and renderer tests will need updating to match the new AST shape. See project/plans/parser-next-steps.md for full context.
steps: Add `BarlineKind`, `Barline`, and update `Bar` and `Row` interfaces in types.ts
Add `OpenBarline` and `CloseBarline` rules to grammar.pegjs covering all barline variants
Update `Row` rule to use `OpenBarline` and store result as `openBarline`
Update `BarTail` rule to use `CloseBarline` and store result as `closeBarline` on the bar
Remove the now-unused `Bar` rule from grammar.pegjs
Run `pnpm build` to regenerate generated.js
Update existing parser tests (parseRow, parseBar, parseSong) to include openBarline/closeBarline in expected AST
Add `barlineSymbol` helper and update `renderRow` in text.ts to emit correct barline symbols
Update existing renderer tests to expect new barline-aware output
Add tests for each new barline kind (double, final, startRepeat, endRepeat, endRepeatStartRepeat, with repeatCount)
Run `pnpm test` and confirm all tests pass

description: Support `%` simile marks that resolve to the previous bar's content at parse time
detail: Add a simile alternative to BarTail in the grammar that stores a temporary marker, then resolve it in the Row action by copying the previous bar's slots. The resolved AST contains only BeatSlot[] with no trace of `%`. Optionally add a `simile` config flag to the renderer to re-emit `%` for consecutive identical bars. See project/plans/parser-next-steps.md for full context.
steps: Add simile alternative to `BarTail` in grammar.pegjs: `"%" _ close:CloseBarline _ { return { type: "bar", simile: true, slots: [], closeBarline: close }; }`
In the Row action, after building the bars array, loop through and replace simile bars with a copy of the previous bar's slots
Run `pnpm build` to regenerate generated.js
Add parser tests: single `%`, multiple consecutive `%`, `%` with non-single barlines
Verify that a simile bar's slots equal the preceding bar's slots in the AST
Optionally add `simile?: boolean` config to TextRendererConfig and implement detection in the renderer
Run `pnpm test` and confirm all tests pass

description: Add a regression test that parses every example .chart file and asserts no errors
detail: Create packages/grigson/src/parser/examples.test.ts that uses globSync to find all *.chart files in the documentation/examples directory and asserts each one parses without throwing. This test should be added last, after all feature tasks complete, so it passes from the start. See project/plans/parser-next-steps.md for full context.
steps: Create `packages/grigson/src/parser/examples.test.ts` using globSync to enumerate all *.chart files
For each file, call `parseSong(readFileSync(path, 'utf8'))` inside an `it` block and expect it not to throw
Run `pnpm test` and confirm all example charts parse cleanly

description: Add 7b5 and - chord quality suffixes to TextMate grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports two quality suffixes missing from the TextMate chord pattern: '7b5' (dom7flat5) and '-' (min7, i.e. C- means Cm7). The current alternation in the chord match regex is 'm7b5|maj7|M7|dim7|m7|dim|m|7'. It must become 'm7b5|maj7|M7|dim7|m7|dim|m|7b5|7|-' with 7b5 before 7 to avoid premature matching. Sync the update to both packages/textmate-grammar/grigson.tmLanguage.json and packages/vscode-extension/syntaxes/grigson.tmLanguage.json.
steps: In the 'chord' repository entry, update the quality capture group regex from 'm7b5|maj7|M7|dim7|m7|dim|m|7' to 'm7b5|maj7|M7|dim7|m7|dim|m|7b5|7|-'
Sync the same change to packages/vscode-extension/syntaxes/grigson.tmLanguage.json
Add syntax-test.chart entries for G7b5 and C- and confirm they highlight correctly

description: Add extended barline variants to TextMate grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports eight barline kinds: | || ||. ||: :|| :||xN :||: :||xN: The TextMate 'barline' repository entry only matches the literal '|'. All multi-character barlines are invisible to the highlighter. Update the match to cover all variants, longest alternatives first, so that :||x3: is not partially matched as :|| or |.
steps: Replace the single-character '\\|' match in the 'barline' repository entry with a regex that matches all variants in longest-first order: :||x\d+:|:||x\d+|:||:|:|||\|\|\.|\|\|:|\|\|(?![:.])|\| — adjust as needed for JSON regex escaping
Sync the same change to packages/vscode-extension/syntaxes/grigson.tmLanguage.json
Add syntax-test.chart rows exercising ||, ||., ||:, :||, :||x2, :||: and verify highlighting

description: Add simile mark (%) pattern to TextMate grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports a % simile mark inside a bar (e.g. | C | % | % |) which copies the previous bar's content. The TextMate grammar has no pattern for %. It should be highlighted similarly to the dot beat-slot, as a punctuation or operator token. Add a 'simile' repository entry and include it in the main pattern list.
steps: Add a 'simile' repository entry with a match regex similar to the dot: (?<=\\||\\s)%(?=\\s|\\||$) and scope 'keyword.operator.simile.grigson'
Include '#simile' in the main pattern list (after '#dot')
Sync to packages/vscode-extension/syntaxes/grigson.tmLanguage.json
Add syntax-test.chart entries with % bars and verify highlighting

description: Add slash chord bass note highlighting to TextMate grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports slash chords such as F/C, Am/E, and C-/Bb, where the text after '/' is a bass note (a Root: note letter + optional accidental). The TextMate chord pattern lookahead (?=\\s|\\||$) stops before the slash, so the '/C' part receives no scope. Extend the chord pattern to optionally match a slash bass, capturing the '/', the bass note letter, and the optional bass accidental in separate capture groups.
steps: Extend the 'chord' match regex to include an optional slash-bass group: (/([A-G])([#b])?) — append after the quality capture and before the lookahead

Add capture entries for groups 4 (/), 5 (bass note letter), and 6 (bass accidental) with appropriate scopes (e.g. punctuation.separator.slash.grigson, constant.other.chord.bass.grigson, constant.other.chord.accidental.grigson)
Sync to packages/vscode-extension/syntaxes/grigson.tmLanguage.json
Add syntax-test.chart entries for F/C, Am/E, C-/Bb and verify all parts highlight

description: Add comment line (#) pattern to TextMate grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports comment lines that begin with # outside front matter (e.g. '# This is a comment'). These lines are silently ignored by the parser. The TextMate grammar has no pattern for comment lines, so they receive no highlighting. Add a 'comment' repository entry matching lines starting with # and give them a comment scope so editors show them greyed out.
steps: Add a 'comment' repository entry with a match of '^#.*$' and name 'comment.line.number-sign.grigson'
Include '#comment' at the top of the main pattern list (before '#frontmatter') so it matches first
Sync to packages/vscode-extension/syntaxes/grigson.tmLanguage.json
Add comment lines to syntax-test.chart and verify they render as comments

description: Add 7b5 and - chord quality tokens to tree-sitter grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports two quality suffixes missing from the tree-sitter quality rule: '7b5' (dom7flat5) and '-' (min7, written as C- meaning Cm7). The current tree-sitter quality choice is ('m7b5', 'maj7', 'M7', 'dim7', 'm7', 'dim', 'm', '7'). Add '7b5' before '7' and '-' after 'm' to maintain longest-match-first ordering.
steps: In grammar.js, update the quality rule choice to include '7b5' (before '7') and '-' (after 'm')
Run tree-sitter generate
Add corpus tests in test/corpus/chords.txt for G7b5 (→ dom7flat5) and C- (→ min7 via dash)
Run tree-sitter test to confirm all corpus tests pass
Update queries/highlights.scm if the new quality tokens need separate highlight captures

description: Add extended barline variants to tree-sitter grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar supports eight barline kinds (|, ||, ||., ||:, :||, :||xN, :||:, :||xN:) for both open and close positions. The tree-sitter grammar's barline rule is just the literal '|'. All multi-character barlines would produce ERROR nodes. Replace the single barline rule with open_barline and close_barline rules (or a unified barline rule with all alternatives in longest-first order), update row and bar_tail to use the appropriate rule, and add corpus tests for each barline kind.
steps: Add open_barline rule covering :||:, :||, ||:, ||, | in longest-first order
Add close_barline rule covering :||x\d+:, :||:, :||x\d+, :||, ||:, ||., ||, | in longest-first order
Update row to use open_barline and bar_tail to use close_barline
Run tree-sitter generate
Add corpus tests in test/corpus/bars.txt for ||, ||., ||:, :||, :||x2, :||: open and close positions
Update queries/highlights.scm to scope open_barline and close_barline
Run tree-sitter test to confirm all corpus tests pass

description: Add simile mark (%) alternative to tree-sitter bar_tail rule
detail: The Peggy grammar supports a % simile mark as an alternative to a full beat slot list inside a bar (e.g. | C | % | % |). The tree-sitter bar_tail rule only handles seq(optional($.time_signature), repeat1($.beat_slot), $.barline). A % in the input would produce an ERROR node. Add a simile_mark rule ('.' is the tree-sitter way — use '%') and a simile_bar_tail alternative, or add choice to bar_tail to handle % as its content.
steps: Add a simile_mark rule: (_$) => '%'
Update bar_tail to be choice(seq(optional($.time_signature), repeat1($.beat_slot), $.barline), seq($.simile_mark, $.barline))
Run tree-sitter generate
Add corpus tests in a new test/corpus/simile.txt covering: single %, multiple consecutive %, % with non-single close barline
Update queries/highlights.scm to scope (simile_mark) as @keyword.operator
Run tree-sitter test to confirm all corpus tests pass

description: Add slash chord bass note to tree-sitter chord rule
detail: The Peggy grammar supports slash chords (e.g. F/C, Am/E, C-/Bb) where a '/' followed by a bass Root is optionally appended to a chord. The tree-sitter chord rule is seq($.note_letter, optional($.accidental), optional($.quality)) with no slash bass. F/C would parse only 'F' as a chord and leave '/C' as an ERROR. Add a slash_bass rule and make it optional in chord.
steps: Add a slash_bass rule: ($) => seq('/', $.note_letter, optional($.accidental))
Update chord to seq($.note_letter, optional($.accidental), optional($.quality), optional($.slash_bass))
Run tree-sitter generate
Add corpus tests in test/corpus/chords.txt for F/C, Am/E, C-/Bb
Update queries/highlights.scm to scope slash_bass parts appropriately
Run tree-sitter test to confirm all corpus tests pass

description: Add comment line (#) rule to tree-sitter grammar
detail: The Peggy grammar silently ignores lines beginning with # outside front matter. The tree-sitter grammar has no comment rule, so any # line in the song body would produce ERROR nodes. Add a comment rule and include it as an alternative at the top level of the song body.
steps: Add a comment rule: (_$) => /#[^\n]*(\n|$)/ (or use token with optional newline)
Update song to include $.comment as an alternative: repeat(choice($.section_label, $.comment, $.row, /\r?\n/))
Run tree-sitter generate
Add corpus tests in a new test/corpus/comments.txt covering: standalone comment, comment before a section label, comment between rows
Update queries/highlights.scm to scope (comment) as @comment
Run tree-sitter test to confirm all corpus tests pass

description: Parse optional key annotation on section labels
detail: Add `key: string | null` to the `Section` interface in `src/parser/types.ts`. Update the `SectionLabel` rule in `grammar.pegjs` to capture an optional ` key: VALUE` suffix after the closing `]` using the existing `FrontMatterValue` rule and the same `isValidKey` predicate used in `FrontMatter`. Store the raw value without applying `normalizeKey` — section.key must be in KEYS-table format (e.g. `'A'`, `'Am'`, `'F# dorian'`) because `detectKey`'s `scores` Map is keyed by `Object.keys(KEYS)`. Update the `SongBody` action to track `pendingKey` alongside `pendingLabel` and include `key: pendingKey` in every section push. Rebuild the generated parser with `pnpm build:grammar`. See project/plans/section-keys.md for full context.
steps: Add `key: string | null` field to the `Section` interface in `src/parser/types.ts`
Update `SectionLabel` in `grammar.pegjs` to match optional `key: VALUE` after `]`, validate with `isValidKey`, and return `{ type: 'sectionLabel', label, key }`
Update `SongBody` action to initialise `pendingKey = null`, set it from `item.key` when a sectionLabel is seen, and include `key: pendingKey` in every `sections.push()`
Run `pnpm build:grammar` to regenerate `generated.js`
Run `pnpm test` — all existing tests should pass (sections without annotations get `key: null`)

In `normaliseSong`, update the call site to pass `sec.key` as the third argument. This lets a section with `[Chorus] key: A` drive enharmonic normalisation toward A major instead of auto-detecting. Depends on section-key-parse (Section.key must exist). See project/plans/section-keys.md for full context.

Change `detectKey(chords, null, config)` inside `normaliseSection` to `detectKey(chords, declaredKey ?? null, config)`
In `normaliseSong`, update the `normaliseSection` call to pass `sec.key` as the third argument

description: Fix semanticChecks ignoring song.meter when computing the effective time signature
detail: The validator's semanticChecks function initialises effectiveTimeSig to 4/4 and only updates it from bar.timeSignature fields. After normaliseSong hoists a uniform inline meter (e.g. (6/8)) to song.meter and strips bar.timeSignature, the validator defaults to 4/4 and emits spurious warnings. Fix by adding a parseMeterString helper that parses a 'N/D' string into a TimeSignature, then initialising effectiveTimeSig from song.meter (falling back to 4/4 when song.meter is null or 'mixed'). Also add two tests to validator.test.ts: one for a front-matter-only meter chart, and one round-trip that normalises a 6/8 chart, renders it, and validates the rendered string. See project/plans/validate-dot-slots.md for full context.
steps: In packages/grigson/src/validator.ts, add parseMeterString(meter: string | null): TimeSignature | null above semanticChecks

description: Capture bar source location in the BarTail grammar rule as a non-enumerable property
detail: Beat-balance warnings always point to line 0:0 because Bar AST nodes carry no location. Fix by capturing Peggy's location() in both BarTail alternatives (the normal bar and the simile % bar) and attaching it to the bar object via Object.defineProperty with enumerable: false. Non-enumerable properties are invisible to vitest's toEqual and JSON.stringify, so existing round-trip tests remain unaffected. After editing the grammar, rebuild the generated parser with pnpm build:grammar. See project/plans/validate-dot-slots.md for full context.
steps: In packages/grigson/src/parser/grammar.pegjs, in BarTail alternative 1 (normal bar), add: const loc = location(); then Object.defineProperty(bar, '_sourceRange', { value: { start: { line: loc.start.line - 1, character: loc.start.column - 1 }, end: { line: loc.end.line - 1, character: loc.end.column - 1 } }, enumerable: false, writable: false, configurable: false }); before return bar
Apply the same Object.defineProperty block to BarTail alternative 2 (the simile % branch), on the bar object before returning it
Run cd packages/grigson && pnpm build:grammar to regenerate generated.js
Run pnpm test — all existing tests pass (non-enumerable property is invisible to toEqual)

description: Use the _sourceRange non-enumerable property in semanticChecks to report warnings at the correct bar location

Add a barRange(bar) helper that reads the non-enumerable _sourceRange via Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor and falls back to zeroRange(). Replace the zeroRange() call in the diagnostics.push inside semanticChecks with barRange(bar). Add three tests covering line-number attribution: bad bar on line 1 reports range.start.line === 1, bad bar on line 0 reports range.start.line === 0, and character is > 0 (bar content starts after the opening barline). Depends on validate-bar-location-grammar. See project/plans/validate-dot-slots.md for full context.
steps: In packages/grigson/src/validator.ts, add barRange(bar: Bar): DiagnosticRange below zeroRange(), using Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(bar, '_sourceRange')?.value ?? zeroRange()
Replace range: zeroRange() with range: barRange(bar) in the diagnostics.push call inside semanticChecks

Add test: 'warning range.start.line is 0 when the bad bar is on the first line' using source '| (5/4) C . G |'

description: Normalizer sets bar.timeSignature on the first bar of uniform-meter songs
detail: Currently, for uniform-meter songs, the normalizer hoists the inline time signature token to song.meter and strips it from the bars. The HTML renderer relies on bar.timeSignature being present to know whether to display a time signature annotation — including at the very start of the chart. The normalizer must be updated to explicitly set bar.timeSignature on the first bar of a song when the song has a declared meter (song.meter is not null). For meter: mixed, the normalizer already preserves inline time sig tokens on each section's first bar, so no change is needed for that case. See project/plans/default-html-renderer.md for full context.

Commit the change

description: Add computeGlobalLayout() to html.ts that computes CSS Grid column assignments for all rows
detail: Before rendering, a single pre-pass over the entire song must compute the global CSS Grid layout. Add a pure function computeGlobalLayout(song) to html.ts that: (1) walks all sections → rows → bars, tracking the active time signature (default 4/4, updated when bar.timeSignature is set); (2) detects Mode 1 (no dot slots → beatsPerSlot = numerator / slots.length) vs Mode 2 (any dot slot → beatsPerSlot = 1 per slot) per bar; (3) builds a per-row layout array giving { col, span } for each barline and slot position; (4) computes globalMaxBeats (maximum row beat total across the song, used as --beat-cols) and globalMinBeatWidth (max of estimatedChordWidth / beatsPerSlot across all chord slots, in em, using ~0.55em per character; slash chords use max(topWidth, bassWidth)). The function returns the layout map and { beatCols, minBeatWidth }. Row beat total = sum of slot counts × beatsPerSlot across all bars, plus 1 for the final barline anchor column. See project/plans/default-html-renderer.md for full context.
steps: Read packages/grigson/src/renderers/html.ts and the Song/Row/Bar AST types to understand the data shapes
Add the computeGlobalLayout(song) function (no changes to rendering yet)
